schools.summary: The verb is a pluperfect ('had spoken'); the account is displaced in time, belonging before the Naaman narrative, and the seven years may not be literal.
Henry: the Shunammite's kindness to the prophet is repaid by a timely warning; the famine, peculiar to Israel, shows God's distinguishing hand.
Gill: Elisha directs the woman whose son he raised to sojourn abroad; she had every reason to trust his word.
Wesley: 'the Lord hath called for a famine' teaches that all afflictions come at God's command; seven years doubles Elijah's famine because Israel was incorrigible.

debates.question: When does this event belong in the chronology?

sides.position: before the Naaman narrative — a displaced, pluperfect notice
the 'seven years' need not be pressed literally

schools.summary: Gill: she obeyed because she had proved the prophet's word in the gift and restoration of her son; past mercy grounds present faith.
Ellicott and Keil: the Philistine lowlands escaped the droughts of the Israelite highlands and traded with foreigners, so grain could be had there; the note sets up her later appeal to the king.
Gill: she returns on the strength of the prophet's word, reckoning the seven years he had fixed to be over.
Ellicott: she journeys from Shunem to Samaria to seek redress, her property having fallen into the hands of strangers — the State or an encroaching neighbour.
Wesley: the forsaken house and land had been taken over by her own kindred.

themes: the king as fountain of justice

schools.summary: Gill: Gehazi is still 'the servant of the man of God,' so this precedes his dismissal, Naaman's cure, and the siege of Samaria.
Keil and Ellicott: the timing is a stroke of providence; Gehazi was not yet a leper, and in any case lepers could converse, so the scene need not follow the Naaman story.
Clarke doubts a king would talk with a disgraced leper and dates the scene after Naaman; Wesley notes the law forbade only dwelling with lepers, not speaking to them.

debates.question: Does this conversation with Gehazi fall before or after the healing of Naaman?

sides.position: before — Gehazi is still Elisha's servant and not yet a leper
after — the king's curiosity questions the now-leprous ex-servant at a distance

schools.summary: Gill: the restored 'dead body' was the Shunammite's son, now the subject of Gehazi's report.
Clarke: a providential occurrence — Gehazi's account, corroborated by the woman herself, moved the king to order her land restored.
Ellicott notes the overlapping tenses and the grown son who came as escort; Keil records the king sending a chamberlain to recover her property and its produce.

themes: the risen son as living proof

schools.summary: Gill: she rehearses the whole miracle — the son given when she was barren, his death, and his restoration through Elisha's prayer — before the king.

Ellicott reads the officer as a eunuch (saris) and the 'fruits' as produce paid from royal stores, implying the land had been annexed to the crown; Keil notes the full restitution of estate and seven years' harvest.
Gill: Elisha likely came to declare that Hazael would be king of Syria, fulfilling the charge given to Elijah.
Ellicott: all reasons for the journey are conjecture; the visit was unexpected and peaceful, so a divine impulse led the prophet, the revelation coming once he arrived.
Henry: no throne is safe from sickness; affliction makes even a heathen king seek the God of Israel and honour the prophet he once hunted.
Clarke: some say Elisha came to lead Gehazi to repentance.

debates.question: Why did Elisha go to Damascus?

sides.position: to anoint or announce Hazael as king, per Elijah's charge
a divine impulse whose reason is not stated; the errand cannot be fixed

themes: the man of God in Syria

schools.summary: Ellicott: Hazael was the king's highest officer; the present follows custom when consulting a seer, and 'go to meet him' means seek an interview, not that Elisha was travelling. Keil flags the Chethib/Keri crux of the oracle.
Clarke marvels at the forty camels' burden; Wesley: the king who bowed in the house of Rimmon in health now enquires of the God of Israel — affliction changes men's thoughts of God's ministers.
Gill: Hazael was the captain general of the Syrian army.

themes: sickness turns a heathen to the true God
a false heart on a true errand

schools.summary: Ellicott: a present of money plus 'every kind of good thing' from Damascus, a great trade centre; the forty camels are a real, ostentatious train, and 'father' was the respectful way to address a prophet.
Gill: the present was customary when a prophet or seer was consulted.
Wesley: 'thy son' — the king who once persecuted Elisha as an enemy now honours him like a father.
Gill: the disease was not mortal — of it he would recover — but he would die a violent death; the Chethib reading 'thou shalt not recover' points the same way.
Ellicott: Elisha speaks in irony, foretelling the lie Hazael will tell, not commanding one; the prophet sees through Hazael and is no accomplice.
Clarke and Wesley: no contradiction — the disease would not kill him (a proof of contingent events), but another hand would take his life.

debates.question: How is 'thou mayest recover, howbeit he shall surely die' to be read?

sides.position: the disease is not fatal, but a violent death by another hand will follow
an ironic prophecy of the flattering lie Hazael will report, not a command to deceive

themes: death not by disease but by murder

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: Elisha fixed his face on Hazael and held the stare until Hazael, conscious his thoughts were read, was disconcerted; Wesley agrees.
Gill: probably Elisha set his face on Hazael and looked at him intently, reading the murderer before him.
Clarke: uncertain whether Elisha or Hazael steels his face; he inclines to the prophet, and notes a Septuagint variant making Hazael present gifts till ashamed.

debates.question: Who 'settled his countenance' and who 'was ashamed'?

sides.position: Elisha fixed his gaze on Hazael until Hazael was ashamed
the reference is uncertain; possibly Elisha steeled his own face before weeping

themes: the man of God outfaces the man of war

schools.summary: Keil, Clarke, and Ellicott anchor the prophecy in its later fulfilment (2 Kings 10, 13) and the prophets, Ellicott noting these were the ordinary cruelties of ancient war, foreseen in advance.
Gill: Hazael supposed the prophet wept for the coming death of Ben-hadad.
Keil and Ellicott read 'is thy servant a dog' as feigned humility and pretended amazement, an exaggerated modesty that betrays the hypocrite; Elisha's announcement strips the disguise.
Gill hesitates: 'dog' means either a cruel creature recoiling from the deed or a mean, abject one incapable of so great a thing — he leans to the latter.
Clarke: Hazael is delighted, not horrified; 'dog' marks his despicable lowliness, and his question expresses surprise that so worthless a man could attain such power.

debates.question: Does Hazael's 'is thy servant a dog?' express horror or ambition?

sides.position: feigned humility masking a murderer's design
astonished delight at the prospect of a crown; 'dog' means lowly, not cruel
either abhorrence of the barbarity or a sense of his own incapacity

outliers.note: Rejects the standard 'horror' reading, arguing (with Patten) that Hazael is thrilled at the prospect of power, 'dog' denoting a despicable, not a cruel, creature.

schools.summary: Ellicott: Hazael repeats Elisha's actual words ('thou wilt live') but suppresses the tone, gesture, and sentence of death; Keil carries the narrative to the murder.

Henry: Hazael cheated his master and belied the prophet, concealing the word of death — the devil's way of rocking men asleep with 'thou shalt surely recover,' robbing the king of warning and Elisha of his credit.
Gill: Hazael came to his master Ben-hadad with the report.

themes: a soft lie before a hard crime
the enemy's work in a friend's voice

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'he' is Hazael; the makber is a plaited quilt that, soaked and laid on the face, suffocates; he rebuts the slander that Elisha incited the murder and cites the Black Obelisk naming Hazael of Damascus.
Clarke and Wesley: a thick woollen cloth spread so closely on the face that the king was smothered and choked.
Gill: it was done the very next day, so hasty was Hazael to be king as the prophet foretold.

themes: the prophet cleared of the crime

schools.summary: Ellicott (with Clarke) treats 'Jehoshaphat being then king of Judah' as a spurious insertion that wrecks the chronology and should be omitted; no genuine co-regency is attested.
Clarke shows the numbers cannot stand and would delete the clause; Wesley instead reads it as a real second co-regency, Jehoshaphat crowning Jehoram near death as David did Solomon.
Henry: parents cannot give grace; Jehoshaphat's errors — marrying his son to Ahab's daughter and crowning him early — ruined him, and he chose Ahab's house for his pattern.
Gill: the reign is dated from Joram of Israel, who began in Jehoshaphat's eighteenth year.

debates.question: Is 'Jehoshaphat being then king of Judah' genuine (a co-regency) or a textual corruption?

sides.position: a spurious anticipation to be omitted; no co-regency existed
a real second co-regency, Jehoshaphat crowning his son before his death

themes: a bad son of a good father

schools.summary: Keil: Joram married Athaliah, transplanted Baal-worship, and murdered his brothers for their treasures; Judah survives only because God preserves David's lamp. Ellicott contrasts the terse annals with the rich Elisha narratives.
Clarke: the eight years were three with his father and five alone, dated by Ussher.
Gill: this is the second accession, in his father's lifetime.
Ellicott: as son-in-law of Ahab and Jezebel, Jehoram promoted the Tyrian Baal and, under Athaliah's influence, slew his six brothers; Michaelis links the reign to Tyre and the founding of Carthage.

Clarke names Athaliah and the Ahab-Jehoshaphat confederacy; Wesley: the unequal marriage was the cause of his posterity's wickedness and calamities — 'those that are ill matched are already half-ruined.'
Gill: he imitated the house of Ahab in idolatry.

themes: a marriage that ruins a house

schools.summary: Ellicott roots the 'light' in the Davidic covenant and weighs 'and to his children'; Keil stresses that the mercy did not cancel chastisement — Edom, Libnah, and a fatal sickness.
Wesley: the 'always' reaches only to the coming of the Messiah, who holds the kingdom forever in his own person; Clarke: a successor in David's family.
Gill: the sparing was not for Joram's merits but for the mercy God assured David.

themes: the promise runs to the Messiah

schools.summary: Ellicott: God let Judah be weakened though not destroyed; Edom, subject since Rehoboam, slew its vassal king and made its own — a defection that is judgment and mercy together.
Gill: Edom had been tributary to Judah since David's day, about a hundred and fifty years.
Henry: the revolt fulfils Isaac's word that Esau would at last break Jacob's yoke, and begins Edom's long enmity seen in Obadiah and Psalm 137.

Keil calls the account obscure; Ellicott suspects a lacuna and reconstructs it — Jehoram, hemmed in, cut his way out by night, but his army fled and the revolt held; Zair is a form of Seir, and Joel 3:19 hints at a massacre of settled Jews.
Clarke: surrounded by the Edomites, Joram cut his way through by night and every man escaped to his tent, unable to press the victory, so Edom's revolt stood.
Gill: Zair is a city in Edom, perhaps the same as Seir.

Ellicott: 'unto this day' dates the source and is confirmed by Assyrian monuments naming an independent king of Edom; Libnah's revolt spread from Edom's success, perhaps aided by a Philistine invasion.
Gill: the revolt held because Joram did not pursue his advantage but returned home.
Henry: Libnah, a priests' city, seceded to preserve its religion because the king had forsaken God and would have forced them to do the same.
Gill: the 'chronicles' meant are the lost state annals of Judah's kings, not the canonical book of Chronicles.
Ellicott (with Clarke): the surviving parallel in 2 Chronicles 21 preserves what the terse compiler omits — Elijah's letter, the invasions, and Joram's fatal disease.
Ellicott: Joram was buried in the city of David but not in the royal tombs — an honour withheld; Keil: Ahaziah began at twenty-two, the parallel's 'forty-two' being a copyist's error.
Gill: Joram 'died as his fathers did.'
Gill: he is also called Jehoahaz, the youngest son of Jehoram.

Henry: a 'little king,' mean in history and vile before God; the shared royal names betray the fatal affinity with Ahab, and Ahaziah's family ties are the occasion of both his wickedness and his fall.

Keil: the parallel's 'forty-two' is a copyist's confusion of the letters for twenty and forty; Ellicott: Athaliah is called 'daughter of Omri' because Omri, not Ahab, is named as the dynasty's founder and byword for wickedness.

Clarke rehearses Lightfoot's harmonization ('son of forty-two years of the house of Omri') but rejects it, holding with Calmet that a transcriber's error is the honest explanation; read twenty-two in both places.
Gill refers to his own solution of the age discrepancy.

debates.question: How is the '22' here reconciled with '42' in 2 Chronicles 22:2?

sides.position: a copyist's error confusing twenty and forty; read twenty-two
'forty-two years of the house of Omri' — a reckoning from Omri on the mother's side

schools.summary: Ellicott marks the threefold repetition of 'the house of Ahab' and the Chronicler's note that his mother counselled him to wickedness; Keil: he reigned in Ahab's spirit because he let Athaliah be his adviser.
Gill: he worshipped both the calves of Jeroboam and Baal, the twin idolatries of Ahab's house.

Keil: the war for Ramoth, which killed Ahab, is providence's occasion for exterminating Omri's house; Ellicott defends (against Ewald) that Ahaziah truly shared the campaign, going at his mother's urging.
Clarke: they went to wrest Ramoth-gilead from the Syrians, the city where Ahab had been slain in the same attempt.
Gill: Joram was Ahaziah's uncle, his mother's brother.

Keil: 'Ramah' is short for Ramoth-gilead; Ellicott: Joram withdrew to Jezreel, the court's seat, by the easier Jordan route, and notes the chapter division wrongly breaks a continuous narrative into chapter 9.
Clarke: at Jezreel Jehu would slay Joram, the blood shed by Ahab and Jezebel visited in the extinction of their family; chronological tangles are copyists' faults.
Gill: Joram withdrew from Ramoth, having taken it and left his army there.

themes: the blood of Ahab's house visited
